question: [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker front-run a victim's bundle call by partially filling one or more of the victim's targeted sell offers (consuming enough units to reduce consumableUnits below the per-offer slice needed), causing every take in the loop to either revert (caught by try/catch) or fill 0 units, so that filledUnits < targetUnits and the final require(filledUnits == targetUnits, OutOfOffers()) reverts the entire bundle, under preconditions where the victim has submitted a bundle with a fixed takes[] array referencing specific offer hashes and the attacker holds enough loan tokens to partially fill those offers directly via Midnight.take? Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.take(victimOffer, ..., partialUnits, attacker, ...) for each offer in victim's takes[] array; victim's bundle call then executes; ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits returns 0 or near-0 for each offer; filledUnits never reaches targetUnits; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: a valid bundle with sufficient on-chain offer capacity must not be permanently blocked by a third party at low cost. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles with attacker cost proportional only to gas plus the partial fill amount (which the attacker can immediately recover by selling back). Proof idea: fuzz over (targetUnits, partialFillFraction) and assert that after attacker partial-fills each offer by partialFillFraction, victim's bundle always reverts with OutOfOffers().

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a permanent DoS on the exact-equality check require(filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets, OutOfOffers()) by exploiting a rounding discrepancy between TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits (which computes units = mulDivDown(target, WAD, buyerPrice)) and Midnight.take's actual buyerAssets computation (units.mulDivUp(buyerPrice, WAD) for sell offers), under preconditions where the settlement fee changes between the TakeAmountsLib.settlementFee view call and the actual take execution within the same transaction (e.g., because touchMarket updates the fee at line 195 and the fee breakpoint is at a boundary), causing the actual buyerAssets to differ from targetFilledBuyerAssets by 1 wei? Call sequence: touchMarket updates settlement fee; TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits computes units using old fee; Midnight.take uses new fee; actual buyerAssets != targetFilledBuyerAssets; require fails. Invariant tested: TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits must produce units such that Midnight.take returns exactly targetBuyerAssets. Scoped impact: DoS against take via buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral for any bundle where settlement fee is at a breakpoint boundary. Proof idea: unit test with maturity exactly at a fee breakpoint, assert filledBuyerAssets != targetFilledBuyerAssets after take.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a permanent DoS on require(filledSellerAssets == targetFilledSellerAssets, OutOfOffers()) by exploiting the rounding asymmetry in TakeAmountsLib.sellerAssetsToUnits (units = mulDivDown(target, WAD, sellerPrice) for buy offers) versus Midnight.take's actual sellerAssets (units.mulDivDown(sellerPrice, WAD) for buy offers), under preconditions where sellerPrice and targetFilledSellerAssets are chosen such that mulDivDown(mulDivDown(target, WAD, sellerPrice), sellerPrice, WAD) < target, causing filledSellerAssets to undershoot targetFilledSellerAssets and the loop to continue past the last offer? Call sequence: attacker crafts takes[] with specific tick/sellerPrice values; loop fills all offers but filledSellerAssets < targetFilledSellerAssets; require fails with OutOfOffers(). Invariant tested: sellerAssetsToUnits inverse must be exact for all valid (sellerPrice, targetSellerAssets) pairs. Scoped impact: DoS against sell-side bundle take for specific price/amount combinations. Proof idea: fuzz over (tick, targetSellerAssets) and assert mulDivDown(mulDivDown(target, WAD, sellerPrice), sellerPrice, WAD) == target for all reachable sellerPrice values from TickLib.tickToPrice.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged maker cause a DoS against a victim taker's bundle by calling Midnight.setConsumed(offer.group, offer.maxUnits, maker) to mark their own offer as fully consumed before the bundle executes, causing ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits to return 0 for that offer (via zeroFloorSub(offer.maxUnits, consumed) = 0), so unitsToTake = min(targetUnits - filledUnits, takes[i].units, 0) = 0, and take(offer, ..., 0, ...) is called as a no-op, under preconditions where the victim's bundle references only offers from that maker? Call sequence: maker calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, maxUnits, maker); victim's bundle executes; consumableUnits returns 0 for all offers; filledUnits stays 0; OutOfOffers() reverts. Invariant tested: a maker must not be able to unilaterally block a taker's bundle at zero cost beyond gas. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles at cost of one setConsumed call. Proof idea: state test asserting that after setConsumed(group, maxUnits), all bundle take functions revert with OutOfOffers().

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget / buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can any caller trigger an out-of-bounds array access revert by passing an empty takes[] array (takes.length == 0), causing takes[0] at lines 62, 64, 129, 131, 193, 195, 264, 266 to revert with a Panic(0x32) before any authorization or parameter check, under preconditions where the caller is the taker or an authorized operator? Invariant tested: bundle functions must not revert with an uninformative panic before authorization checks. Scoped impact: DoS against any bundle entry point; also masks authorization errors. [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's ERC2612 permit signature (consuming it via a direct IERC20Permit.permit call before the bundle executes), so that the pullToken call at line 66 falls through to SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom which then reverts because the victim has not separately approved the bundler, under preconditions where the victim relies solely on the ERC2612 permit path (PermitKind.ERC2612) and has not pre-approved MidnightBundles? Call sequence: attacker calls loanToken.permit(victim, bundler, amount, deadline, v, r, s); victim's bundle executes; permit try/catch silently ignores the already-consumed permit; safeTransferFrom reverts due to insufficient allowance. Invariant tested: permit front-running must not permanently block bundle execution when the user has not pre-approved. Scoped impact: DoS against take and repay via MidnightBundles at cost of one permit call. Proof idea: unit test where permit is consumed before bundle call, assert bundle reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's Permit2 signature (consuming it via IPermit2.permitTransferFrom before the bundle executes), causing the pullToken Permit2 branch at lines 388-394 to revert with an InvalidNonce or SignatureExpired error that is NOT caught by a try/catch (unlike ERC2612), reverting the entire bundle, under preconditions where the victim uses PermitKind.Permit2 and the attacker can observe the signature in the mempool? Scoped impact: DoS against take and repay via MidnightBundles at cost of one Permit2 call. [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by making the seller of a targeted offer liquidatable (e.g., by manipulating oracle price or waiting for price drop) before the bundle executes, causing Midnight.take to revert with SellerIsLiquidatable (line 444 in Midnight.sol uses tExchange for liquidation lock, but the actual check is in take), which is caught by the try/catch at line 85, so the offer is silently skipped, and if all offers are skipped, OutOfOffers() reverts the bundle, under preconditions where the victim's bundle targets sell offers from a single maker/seller who can be made liquidatable? Invariant tested: a taker's bundle must not be blockable by making the maker's position unhealthy. Scoped impact: DoS against take via MidnightBundles. [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral / buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by waiting for all targeted offers to expire (offer.expiry < block.timestamp), causing Midnight.take to revert with OfferExpired() for each offer, which is caught by the try/catch, so all offers are silently skipped and OutOfOffers() reverts the bundle, under preconditions where the victim's bundle references offers with short expiry windows and the attacker can delay the transaction (e.g., via mempool manipulation or simply waiting)? Proof idea: fuzz over (expiry, block.timestamp) and assert bundle reverts when all offers are expired.

[File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to change the market's tick spacing (via Midnight.setMarketTickSpacing) to a value that makes the targeted offer's tick inaccessible (offer.tick % newTickSpacing != 0), causing Midnight.take to revert with TickNotAccessible() for each offer, which is caught by the try/catch, so all offers are skipped and OutOfOffers() reverts, under preconditions where the attacker is the tickSpacingSetter and the targeted offers are at ticks that are not multiples of the new spacing? [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to fill all targeted offers to exactly their maxUnits capacity (consumed[maker][group] == offer.maxUnits), causing ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits to return 0 for each offer, so unitsToTake = 0 for all offers, and take(offer, ..., 0, ...) is called as a no-op (or reverts with ConsumedUnits()), all caught by try/catch, and OutOfOffers() reverts the bundle, under preconditions where the attacker has enough loan tokens to fully consume all targeted offers? [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithAssetsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by exploiting the loop exit condition (filledBuyerAssets < targetFilledBuyerAssets) combined with the exact equality require(filledBuyerAssets == targetFilledBuyerAssets), where a rounding overshoot in the last take causes filledBuyerAssets to exceed targetFilledBuyerAssets, causing the loop to exit early (condition false) but the require to fail, under preconditions where TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits computes units such that Midnight.take returns buyerAssets > targetFilledBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets due to mulDivUp rounding in Midnight.take line 363? [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by exploiting the referral fee gross-up formula referralFeeAssets = targetSellerAssets.mulDivDown(referralFeePct, WAD - referralFeePct) at line 277, where a large targetSellerAssets combined with referralFeePct close to WAD causes targetFilledSellerAssets = targetSellerAssets + referralFeeAssets to overflow uint256, reverting the entire bundle before any takes are attempted, under preconditions where the caller passes referralFeePct = WAD - 1 and targetSellerAssets > 1? [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by crafting a scenario where filledBuyerAssets + referralFeeAssets > maxBuyerAssets at line 104, causing an arithmetic underflow revert in SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(loanToken, msg.sender, maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets), under preconditions where referralFeePct is close to WAD and the actual filledBuyerAssets is close to maxBuyerAssets, so that referralFeeAssets = filledBuyerAssets * referralFeePct / (WAD - referralFeePct) exceeds maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets? [File: 'src/periphery/MidnightBundles.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a DoS by front-running the victim's bundle to make the targeted offer's ratifier fail (e.g., by revoking the maker's authorization of the ratifier via Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ratifier, false, maker)), causing Midnight.take to revert with RatifierUnauthorized() for each offer, which is caught by try/catch, all offers are skipped, and OutOfOffers() reverts, under preconditions where the maker has authorized the ratifier and the attacker is the maker or an authorized operator of the maker?
